speaker: interviewer
text: It's February the 13th, 1996. The survivor is Ruth Foster. Her maiden name was Heilbronn. Bernice Krantz is the interviewer. We're in London, England, and we're speaking English. My name is Bernice Krantz. It's February the 13th, 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Ruth Foster, and we are in Winchmore Hill, London, England. Can you please give me your name and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Ruth Foster. R-U-T-H for Ruth, Foster, F-O-S-T-E-R. My maiden name is Heilbronn. H-E-I-L-B-R-O-N-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Did you ever have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was married under the name Freudenheim. F-R-E-U-D-E-N-H-E-I-M. My husband changed his name by deed poll to Foster.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please give me your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: My birth date is the 14th of November, 1922.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age now?

speaker: interviewee
text: My age now? [LAUGHING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Just in case they can't work it out.

speaker: interviewee
text: My age is 73.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And the city and the country you were born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Lingen. It's a small town on the Dutch border of Germany. L-I-N-G-E-N. And the country of Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Can we start by talking about your early childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: My childhood was very happy. I was an only child. Rather spoiled. My parents were very comfortable financially. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They were orthodox, but on the other hand, rather liberal and assimilated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Did you have grandparents living--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had only--

speaker: interviewer
text: --in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: --only two grandmothers living. One lived in Holland, and one in Germany, very near to Lingen. It was my father's mother, and my grandmother from my mother's side lived in Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see much of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every second Sunday, we saw the grandmother who lived near us-- my father's mother. And the other grandmother we saw during the summer holidays, sometimes Christmastime. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there many Jewish people living in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my hometown, there were 20 Jewish families. 100 souls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So you all lived close together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were sort of scattered in the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was a synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a small synagogue, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We had no rabbi, and also no religious-- we had a small Jewish school, and a Hebrew teacher came twice a week on Wednesdays and Sunday afternoons, which we detested, of course. And my father was the president of the synagogue for about 20 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did he take services?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Somebody else took the service. One of the congregants. And I have very happy memories of the Jewish holidays, when my father wore a top hat, and with the top hat, he went through the streets to the synagogue. And the people knew it. It was a Jewish holiday, and the Jews celebrate. They go to the synagogues. They wear top hats and so on. They were the Jewish men, who were on the committee and so on. They wore top hats, and it was quite festive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And very special.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very special.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. What about school?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a part of the Jewish people, they were very happy in this town and all made a comfortable living. I went to a German school. It was an elementary school at first. You start in Germany at the age of seven. And when I was 11, I went to the gymnasium. I was at the gymnasium until 1936, when there was a universal rule in Germany-- a law was passed-- that no Jewish scholars could attend any more German schools, nor gymnasiums, nor universities.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how old were you at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '36, I was 15. 14. 14. And my mother didn't want me to stay at home, so I went to an aunt of mine, a sister of my father, who had a very big estate. An enormous estate with horse dealers. And so I learned the rough work there. I had to work. I had to scrub. I had to clean. And she had seven sons. They all perished. I mean, that comes afterwards. While I was there, my mother-- my parents put my name down for a Jewish school in Berlin. And while I was waiting to get there, I was with this aunt and uncle and the seven cousins, so I learned how to work and how to scrub and whatever, which didn't do me any harm.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of work did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a cattle dealer, and we had a grain merchant business. A very big one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother work with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my mother was just housewife and mother and hostess. She did a lot of charity work because in Lingen was a very big prison. And during the Hitler time, lots of Jewish prisoners were there for racial diff-- because there were the Nuremberg Laws. You know? If they had a German girlfriend or German wives, they were arrested and sent to Lingen, into the prison, or some were dealing in currency and were caught at the borders. Some even tried to get across illegally into Holland, being so close to Holland. They were arrested and put into this prison. And my mother was on very good terms with the prison warden. Warden. And he told her, we have five Jewish men, or four Jewish men, and they were living still kosher at the time. So she would cook for them and bring them the food. She never saw them, but she left it at the prison gates. So that was my mother. And we always had visitors at home and lots of cousins and uncles and aunts. Our house was always bustling with life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any hobbies or interests as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: I belonged, as a child, to the local sports club. I won a few prizes as a child in running and hurdling. And I belonged to the local choir, which all finished abruptly in '36.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you enjoyed singing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I enjoyed singing, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do solos, or were you in--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in a choir. In a choir, yes. Solos I did when we went, for instance, Christmas to the grandmother on father's side, in the small town. The neighbors had all their Christmas trees. They were very strictly Catholics. That area was strictly Catholic. I would go and sing the Christmas carols under the Christmas tree, and I enjoyed it. My mother didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I never-- that was the only solo singing I ever did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the laws came in 1936, were you aware of any anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't at all. We were-- neither of us were aware of any anti-Semitism.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it because you lived in a friendly town, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in a friendly town. We had two maids. See, one had to leave because she was under 45. She was not allowed to be in the-- that was the law, not just our Maria, you know, but they all had to leave Jewish households. And the older one, which was already nearly 50, she could stay. And she stayed on. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My father belonged also to a male choir in the town and to a-- now, this is very common to Germany. I couldn't really explain. They call it schutzen fein, like a rifle. You would say a rifle club. But only the eldest of the town and the people who could afford to belong to it belonged to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was quite elitist?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was quite elitist. And twice a year, they chose a king that was a schuetzenkoenig, the king. So one year, my father was the king, and I had a picture-- a cousin of mine had taken a picture when he was on the horse in his uniform. You know? Proper German, even though, at heart, very, very Jewish and very orthodox. We had a very orthodox household. And so much so that my mother didn't-- only ate-- never touched the meat or anything in the camp or in the ghettos.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was more liberal where that was concerned.

speaker: interviewer
text: So although he was very Jewish, he was very patriotic as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was patriotic. He served as a soldier in the first World War. He won two distinctions, the Iron Cross First Grade and Second Grade, for [? Verdure ?] in France. So did all his brothers. And he served for two years previous to war with the Hussars, which was also an elite regiment.

speaker: interviewer
text: He had no reason to-- not to be patriotic?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they lived there since 1680. As I said, it's amazing that they didn't integrate in-- in the German society more. I mean, intermarriage and so on. That was out. They just-- And, as you see on one of those memorial stones in Lengerich, where my father came from, there were only Heilbronns since 1680.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Quite amazing. So what was the first signs of--

speaker: interviewee
text: The first sign was that in the gymnasium, we had a very nice headmistress. She was disposed of by one of the teachers who was a very great Nazi, and she became the headmistress of the--

speaker: interviewer
text: So the one who was asked to leave was Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no?

speaker: interviewee
text: No Jewish teachers. Nothing. No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was she--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a German gymnasium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So why did she--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because she didn't conform with the-- with the regime. She was anti-Nazi. Not openly, but she didn't want to conform to their rules. So she was sacked. And this Nazi teacher, she became the headmistress. She was, at the same, time my [? fore ?] mistress. She made me sit separately in the classroom. There were two rows of benches left vacant, and I was sitting against the wall.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were the only Jewish girl in your--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the only Jewish girl at the gymnasium. She forbade all the other girls-- who were friends up till then of mine-- to speak to me in the intervals, when we had--

speaker: interviewer
text: A break.

speaker: interviewee
text: --break. Some of the teachers took pity on me, and they came over and talked to me, but the headmistress was not allowed to see it. And then, in '37, I had to leave the school. Like I said before, all Jewish scholars had to leave universities--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. How did you feel when they made you sit at the back of the class?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very isolated and very conscious. Very self-conscious, what happened, sort of, to me overnight. Friends that used to pick me up in the mornings, we used to go to school together, we would go home after school together, dropped me, not because it was their conviction, but they were afraid the teacher might see them, that they had any contact with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you not want to leave the school at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to leave the school. It was law.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, I know, but you said-- how long was it between--

speaker: interviewee
text: There must've been a few months.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you did suffer that situation?

speaker: interviewee
text: I suffered that situation for quite a few months. And this teacher would bring in pictures, big posters, with Jews with big noses, flat feet, curly hair, and she would say, this is a typical Jew. Have a look at them. These are the parasites. And one of the girls, one of the scholars got up, and she said, but look at Ruth Heilbronn and look at the others. They don't look like that. But this is the origin of a typical Jew. This was her attitude. And this girl who stood up and said that none of us looked like those posters, I'm still friendly with her. He father was our local dentist, and he was a very decent-- never sent any bills to the Jewish patients. But that also had to be done secretly. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I then went to Berlin. I went to this Jewish school.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was it for you to travel?

speaker: interviewee
text: It took about four hours by train to travel to Berlin. Berlin is the middle-- it's the northern part of the middle of Germany, and we lived right--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- you didn't come home every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I stayed there. I was in a-- in a-- in a Jewish boarding school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had to leave home.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to leave home.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't mind it because I was with Jewish girls and boys in the seminar, and I went to school and did my O levels. And the other boys and girls, they went to Hasharat, which was preparation for kibbutzim for Palestine, at that time. They worked on the land or in nurseries, like for the garden. Garden nurseries, not-- not babies. Not children's nurseries. Nurseries-- Some girls went to learn hairdressing. And I wanted to be a nurse and a kindergarten teacher. I thought, if I go to Palestine-- I was raised Zionistic-- there is something I can do. Because you were not allowed to study. I would have loved to study medicine. You were not allowed to study anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these were the only options we had, either dressmakers, hairdressers, or working on the land. So my parents chose that for me. But for that, you had to have the-- had O levels, which I took at this Jewish school. It's called-- it was called the [GERMAN] in Berlin. And I'm still now a member of the [GERMAN]. It's now again a school. So how old were you when you took the O levels? 16?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 16. That's right. And then I started-- it's a Jewish hospital in Berlin, but living at this Jewish seminar. Yes, it's a-- it's a boarding school. And going there, you know, for lessons, and for training, and at the same time visiting kindergartens. And so if you got a-- hopefully you got a dual diploma as nurse and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Teacher.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and kindergarten teacher. But it never came to that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you lived in Berlin-- and obviously you were quite cushioned, being in a Jewish environment-- when you were out and about, did you have problems?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't look Jewish, and we sort of-- we disappeared in the masses.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you didn't see any anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not directly. Not directly. Because we were always in a group of ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course we heard about it. My mother wrote about it because things had changed in my hometown as well. And my uncles and aunts, they lived very near the Dutch border. They had-- the Jews had to be evacuated just before the war broke out in case they were spies. And they lived under terrible conditions, these uncles and aunts. They went to bigger towns. So they had to leave everything overnight. And then-- so I knew what was going on. And in other towns, Jewish people had-- Jewish families had their windows broken every night. No sooner did you have them repaired than stones flew through them again. And lots of atrocities went on. And my father's business and everybody else's business came to a standstill because people were afraid to deal with Jewish-- Jewish people. And it was very hard. Financially, it became very, very hard. But we still stayed in our-- in my-- in our house, and people came who tried to flee over the Dutch border. You know, my parents took them in, you know, Jewish people from other parts of Germany. They tried to, at night, to cross the borders, and they waited for a few days, and it was convenient for them to slip across and my parents--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it difficult to cross the border?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very difficult. Very few made it. They got arrested and were sent to this prison in-- in-- in my hometown. So it was quite-- in Berlin, we didn't notice it so much. We were in an enclave. We were amongst-- I was amongst Jewish boys and girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you're young, you don't feel it if it doesn't sort of touch you directly. It touched my parents, of course, because, financially and morally, it was-- was-- was-- very-- neighbors who were-- were good to us and-- and friends in the town, they ignored us. They had to ignore us, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was very, very hard to accept it. See, where, for instance, in Berlin, I was a stranger. I could go in a shop and-- and-- and up to a certain time still. But not--

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't know who we were. But this changed again in 1938. In November '38, when the Kristallnacht came. We didn't know anything-- we had heard that the synagogues in Berlin were burning, and I had a telegram from my mother, 'come home as soon as possible. Father has been arrested, and the synagogue has been burned down.'

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 16 at the time. Yes, 16. I turned 16 on the 14th of November, and this was the 9th, so I would have been 16. I came home, and my mother and all the Jewish women were in a terrible state. All their husbands had been arrested and been sent to Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewee
text: Whereas [? Lingen, ?] in my hometown, they did it very discreetly. The police were ashamed, as they knew the Jewish inhabitants for so many years and lived on good terms with them. Suddenly, for no reason whatsoever, they had to arrest them and send them in-- in-- in lorries to Buchenwald. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father, as I said before, was a soldier in the first World War. And I had the bright idea to go to the CID, which was the equivalent of the German CID, which was in Osnabrueck. So I took his military passport, which he still had, and his medals, and I said he was a soldier in the first World War. And the Kaiser-- my father always used to say, the Kaiser told the troops, when he inspected the troops, and as my father was in one of the elite troops-- I'll say it first in German. [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Germany will be forever grateful for what you are doing for your fatherland. So I said this to the CID officer. And I said, and look what happened to my father and so many other former German soldiers of Jewish race. And they passed a law, all those who were-- going at the time, I was told, passed a law that those who were soldiers in the first World War should be released immediately. My father came home after a few weeks from Buchenwald, and so did a few others who were also soldiers in the first World War. And the others had to stay for much longer in Buchenwald under terrible conditions. They were not allowed to talk about it. They were pressed to emigrate. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Germans didn't want to keep the Jews in Germany anymore. Whoever had a chance to emigrate emigrated. My poor father went to Hamburg-- that's one of the Hansa towns-- to get an affidavit for Siam. When I hear 'The King and I,' I think often, pull the affidavit my poor father got for the family to go to Siam.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why Siam?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the only one where the Jews could still go more or less immediately. Otherwise you would have to have visas and there was-- to America, you had to-- there was a-- was a waiting list. You know, you had to wait your quota and so on. But Siam would accept you more or less immediately. So Siam we were supposed to go. But now to go to Siam, you have to sell your stuff. You have to prepare. And by-- by the time we were ready to go to Siam, the war had broken out.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your father when he came back from Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible. I've got the pictures. There's the only picture of him was he was shaven, and-- his head was shaven, and he was tortured. And because he stood up, he said, [NON-ENGLISH]. Because the wardens were Germans and Poles, which we later encountered in the-- encountered in the concentration camp. Where with-- they had already those uniforms. The striped uniforms. And had different triangles on their jackets. Green ones were for the [GERMAN] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] for the worst criminals. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And mauve were for homosexuals. Pink was for [GERMAN], Jehovah's Witnesses. So but they were not so bad, but the ones with the green-- green triangles, they were the ones who were murderers and-- and the greatest crooks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were in charge of the prisoners. Directly in charge. They were the kapos. Kapo is camp police. Kapo is an abbreviation for camp police, but camp written with a K.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so my father was tortured. He was kicked so much so that he-- for-- until the rest of his life, he walked with a limp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They hit a nerve, and the muscles sort of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Withered.

speaker: interviewee
text: Withered. That's right. And he walked with that to the end of his life, with a limp, because of that. So he came back from Buchenwald in terrible pain, and he walked with a limp until the rest of this life. And of course, then-- from then on, it went from bad to worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did he actually come back?

speaker: interviewee
text: He came back about Christmas of-- of '38. Just around about Christmastime. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was there about a month, or six weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: All over Germany, the Germans were not-- the Jews were not allowed to live near Germans or with Germans in the same house. So they erected Jewish houses. They called it Judenhause.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had to leave our big house and had to go into a very small Jewish house with four families. So we were-- lived there. So there were three Jewish houses [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where the Jews-- the remaining Jews were put into.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a curfew. You couldn't go out before 7:00 in the morning or not until after 7:00 in the evenings. And so these were like enforced ghettos, but in the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you get food?

speaker: interviewee
text: That time, already Hitler had brought in Russian cards, all in preparation for the war. We got special Russian cards with a J on it. J for Jude, for Jew. And only certain shops were allowed to sell to Jews. But, as we were very well known, farmers would bring us food at night. Put it over the fence. And the local bakers would send us food-- bread and so on, so much so that my mother was able to make parcels to send to her brothers and sisters who were living in the big towns and under great hardships. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was, morally, very, very bad. But at that time, physically, not so bad. We had to eat, and we were together. We knew where we were. You know? We knew that the uncles were there, and the cousins were there, and the-- my grandmother had died in the meantime. Both grandmothers had died in '33. One in '33, one in '34. Let me--
